questions.question: Injury caused directly by the blast pressure wave is classified as:
questions.rationale: Primary blast injury is caused by the overpressure wave itself, targeting air-filled organs (ears, lungs, GI tract).

questions.question: A casualty struck by bolts and glass propelled by an explosion has which mechanism of injury?
questions.rationale: Secondary blast injury — fragments and debris — is also the most common cause of injury and death in bombings.

questions.question: A victim thrown by the blast against a concrete pillar suffers which mechanism?
questions.rationale: Tertiary injury results from bodily displacement — being thrown against objects or the ground.

questions.question: Radiological material packed around an explosive ('dirty bomb') produces which class of injury?
questions.rationale: Quinary injuries come from additives to the device — radiological, chemical, or biological contaminants.

questions.question: Which organs are most vulnerable to the primary blast wave?
options: A. Solid organs — liver and spleen
B. Air-filled organs — ears, lungs, GI tract
questions.rationale: The pressure wave does its work at air-tissue interfaces: tympanic membranes, alveoli, and bowel.

questions.question: A blast survivor from inside a bus develops dyspnea, falling SpO2, and hemoptysis 2 hours later. Most likely diagnosis?
options: B. Blast lung (primary pulmonary blast injury)
questions.rationale: Enclosed-space blast plus delayed respiratory deterioration with hemoptysis is classic blast lung — pulmonary contusion and alveolar disruption that can present hours after exposure.

questions.question: When ventilating a patient with suspected blast lung, the provider should:
options: A. Use maximal pressures to recruit the lungs
B. Use the lowest effective pressures and monitor for pneumothorax and air embolism
questions.rationale: Positive pressure can extend alveolar rupture, causing tension pneumothorax or arterial air embolism — gentle ventilation is the rule.

questions.question: An intact tympanic membrane after a blast means:
options: A. No primary blast injury occurred
B. Other primary blast injuries (lung, GI) are still possible — TM status is not a reliable rule-out
C. The patient can be discharged immediately
questions.rationale: TM rupture is the most common primary injury, but its absence does not exclude blast lung or GI injury — clinical assessment and observation still apply.

questions.question: Why do enclosed-space explosions cause worse primary blast injury?
options: B. Reflected pressure waves multiply the blast dose delivered to victims
D. They do not — open air is worse
questions.rationale: Walls reflect and re-reflect the pressure wave, so victims receive multiple wave exposures, raising primary injury incidence and severity.

questions.question: Minutes after a downtown bombing, the nearest ED receives 25 walking wounded. What should hospital command anticipate?
options: A. The event is over — these are all the casualties
B. The critical casualties will arrive later (30–60 min) by EMS; capacity must be reserved for that second wave
C. No more patients will come
D. Only pediatric patients will follow
questions.rationale: Bombing arrivals are 'upside-down': self-transporting minor casualties come first, while the critical ones — needing extrication and EMS — arrive later into an already-crowded ED unless capacity is protected.

questions.question: The greatest cause of PREVENTABLE death in blast and penetrating mass casualties is:
questions.rationale: Extremity exsanguination is rapidly fatal and rapidly fixable — early tourniquets (including bystander Stop the Bleed response) save the most salvageable lives.

questions.question: At a confirmed intentional bombing, EMS should initially:
options: A. Rush straight to the epicenter
B. Stage until law enforcement addresses secondary devices, then operate under unified command
questions.rationale: Secondary devices target responders. Staging, perimeter control, and unified command with law enforcement precede casualty care in the hot area.

questions.question: A blast survivor suddenly develops focal weakness and then collapses. A key blast-specific cause to consider is:
options: A. Arterial air embolism from disrupted alveoli
questions.rationale: Alveolar disruption can admit air into pulmonary veins and the arterial circulation, causing stroke-like deficits, myocardial ischemia, or sudden death.

questions.question: Burns, crush from structural collapse, and smoke inhalation at a bombing are classified as:
questions.rationale: Quaternary covers all explosion-related injuries not in the first three classes — burns, crush, inhalation, and exacerbated chronic disease.

questions.question: During the warm-zone phase of an active-violence response, the care priority is:
options: B. Rapid hemorrhage control (tourniquets/packing) before movement to the cold zone
questions.rationale: Phased care: suppress threat (hot), control life-threatening bleeding with seconds-long interventions (warm), full care once in the cold zone.

questions.question: Which blast casualty most needs prolonged observation despite looking well?
options: A. A bystander 500 m away with no symptoms
B. A survivor from within the enclosed blast space with ringing ears but normal vitals
C. A responder who arrived after detonation
D. A patient with an isolated small hand laceration from 200 m away
questions.rationale: Significant (especially enclosed-space) exposure risks delayed blast lung and GI injury; TM symptoms mark real wave exposure. Observation catches the late presenters.
